REASONS FOR DECISION
INTRODUCTION
[1] XXXX XXXX (the "principal claimant" born XXXX XXXX, 1994), XXXX XXXX (the "female claimant" born XXXX XXXX XXXX 1996) and their children (the "minor claimants"), XXXX XXXX (born XXXX XXXX, 2014) and XXXX XXXX (born XXXX XXXX XXXX 2012), all claim to be citizens of the Slovak Republic (Slovakia) seeking refugee protection pursuant to subsections 96 and 97(1) of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act.1
[2] I heard these claims jointly pursuant to Rule 55 of the Refugee Protection Division Rules2 and maintained the designation of the principal claimant as the representative for the minor claimants, pursuant to subsection 167(2) of the Act and Rule 20.
Allegations
[3] The complete story alleging the basis of the claimants' fears in Slovakia is captured in the principal claimant's Basis of Claim Form (BoC).3 The following outlines their allegations.4
[4] The adult claimants are Roma and raised by their respective families in Roma traditions and culture. They are raising the minor claimants in a similar fashion.
[5] In XXXX 2011, the adult claimants were attacked by five skinheads (fascist Slovaks) and went to the hospital as a result. The claimant reported the attack to the police who were dismissive to the point one officer kicked the principal claimant. The claimant was not able to obtain a corroborating report from either the hospital or the police.
[6] The claimants' village was invaded by skinheads in or about XXXX or XXXX 2012.
[7] The female claimant was discriminated against by health care workers and not provided proper care, especially for her son. This became acute because of the diagnosis and prognosis of the minor claimant, XXXX. He suffers from XXXX, a XXXX XXXX XXXX, which, if untreated in infancy, results in irreversible XXXX XXXX.
[8] The adult claimants were placed in segregated classes or schools and, for that reason, did not receive a proper education. They fear that their children will face the same problem.
[9] Throughout their lives the claimants have been subjected to threats, ridicule, humiliation and systemic discrimination amounting to persecution.
DETERMINATION
[10] The panel determines that the claimants are not Convention refugees and not persons in need of protection. I also find5 that should the claimants return to their home country, they would not be subjected to risk to life or to cruel and unusual treatment or punishment, personally. Further, on substantial grounds, I find there is no danger of torture.
ANALYSIS
Background
[11] There is widespread discrimination against Roma in Slovakia; "of that there can be no doubt".6 However, the issue in this claim is whether there is a reasonable chance these claimants
will be persecuted or whether, on a balance of probabilities, they face a risk to life or other serious harm, should they return to Slovakia.
[12] Societal discrimination of the Roma and individuals of non-European ethnicity is common in Slovakia.7 Roma are the second largest population and there were racially motivated attacks on minorities, including the Roma. The panel notes that nationalist sentiment is increasingly directed against Slovakia's Roma, a minority of 400,000 in the country of 5.4 million who live on the fringes of society, suffering from poverty, poor education and limited job prospects.
[13] Not all Roma in Slovakia face persecution or other serious harm. Each claim depends on the personal history of the claimant, keeping in mind the conditions in Slovakia.8
[14] Racial violence, including incitement to racial hatred, is a crime in Slovakia. The Criminal Code punishes crimes related to extremism. In 2011, a new crime was introduced in the Criminal Code that punishes denying, challenging, approving or any attempts to justify the holocaust or crimes based on Fascist and Communist ideology. Based on the amendment to the Code of Criminal Procedure effective from 1 August 2013, an investigation of crimes related to extremism is obligatory conducted by investigators of the police force (until now members of the police force).
[15] The authorities investigate but this is dependent upon the jurisdiction of the attacks. As of November 2012, the authorities had received 105 reports of violent crimes with racial or extremist motives, 27 cases involved violence against groups. Roma were singled out for violence and recently the former head of the anti-Roma party which was banned in Slovakia, an accused neo-Nazi won a local election as the lead councillor in the central Slovakia area of Balog.9 In May, the UN Committee on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights (CESCR) criticized Slovakia's failure to
take measures to combat discrimination against Roma in education, employment, health and housing.10
Several sources report on police mistreatment of Roma (Council of Europe Feb. 2012, 76; ERRC Mar. 2011, 28; UN 20 Apr. 2011, para. 8; US 24 May 2012, 2), including "racist attacks" by police (UN 20 Apr. 2011, para. 8) and abuse of Roma suspects "during arrest and while in custody" (US 24 May 2012, 2). The Council of Europe indicates that due to the Slovak Republic's data collection, it is not possible to disaggregate statistics on offenses committed against Roma and monitor the follow-up by law enforcement authorities (20 Dec. 2011, 10). For information regarding police oversight mechanisms, see SVK104115.E.
Sources provide the 2009 example in which police officers in Košice detained six Romani boys, forced them to strip, hit and kiss each other, threatened them with loaded guns, unleashed police dogs on them, and yelled anti-Roma statements at them (ERRC Mar. 2011, 28; Council of Europe Feb. 2012, 77). Six police officers and four senior officials were reportedly dismissed following the incident (ibid.). The ERRC states that three officers were still employed by the police force at the time of the first court hearing on 26 August 2010 (Mar. 2011, 28). The court case against the police officers involved in the incident was reportedly pending at the end of 2011 (AI 2012; US 24 May 2012, 2)11.
[16] The panel notes that the government has taken proactive steps to address the issues of discrimination and violence targeting minorities, in particular the Roma.
The National Roma Integration Strategy up to 2020 was adopted by the government in January 2012, in accordance with its obligations as a member of the EU, and focuses on four main areas: education, employment, healthcare, and housing (UN 24 May 2012). According to the government, the goals of the strategy are "to halt the segregation of Roma communities; to facilitate a significant positive turn in the social inclusion of Roma communities; to foster non-discrimination; and to change the attitude of the majority population toward the Roma minority" (Slovak Republic [2011]). Sources indicate that Roma communities were consulted throughout the development process of the strategy (OSF 14 June 2012; UN 24 May 2012). According to the OSF representative, the integration strategy is a "great" policy with "concrete measures, financial commitments, and time frames" (14 June 2012). According to the United Nations Development Programme (UNDP), the strategy includes a regular monitoring mechanism (UN 24 May 2012). In correspondence with the Research Directorate, an official at the Embassy of the Slovak Republic in Ottawa indicated that as a member of the Decade of Roma Inclusion 2005-2015, the Slovak Republic regularly submits progress reports (Slovak Republic 13 June 2012).12
[17] Slovakia's Anti-Discrimination Act, which was adopted in 2004, prohibits direct and indirect discrimination on the grounds of race, nationality, ethnic origin, among others, in the fields of education, social security, employment, healthcare, and access to goods and services. According to the Council of Europe, the Act is "largely under-implemented" due to inadequate awareness of discrimination of the Act among the legal profession, lengthy court proceedings, and a "reported reluctance to [grant] meaningful compensation".13 The documentary evidence states that Slovakia has strengthened the Anti-discrimination Act as of April 1, 2013 which extends the definition of indirect discrimination to include the threat of discrimination, allowing the authorities to take affirmative action to the local level and private entities with a view to promote affirmative action.
[18] The objective evidence makes clear that the authorities in Slovakia have taken a plethora of measures to mitigate the discrimination faced by the Roma. The government has funded education, health care, employment and improved living conditions for the Roma. Slovakia pays appropriate attention to the social inclusion of Roma. Support of social inclusion of persons endangered by social exclusion or socially excluded persons through the development of services with special focus on marginalized Roma communities is among priorities of the Operational Programme Employment and Social Inclusion (2007-2013). Despite these efforts by the Slovak government, statistics indicate that Roma are still underemployed and less educated. The Government has set objectives focused on the improvement of access to work opportunities with particular emphasis on non-discriminatory access to the labour market as well as on active policies and programmes directed to the labour market, education, continuing education, and the support of self-employment activities.
[19] I note that the information is mixed as it relates to the Roma in Slovakia, there are problems in certain areas including in the election of an anti-Roma governor,14 the violence by extremists and other violent entities that specifically target Roma's and other minorities.15 Overall I find there is evidence of a concrete action taken on the part of the police to offer protection to the Roma and other minorities. The panel notes that the police and judiciary are pursuing those that target Roma and other minorities:
As of November authorities received 105 reports of violent crimes with a racial or extremist motive, and 27 cases of violence against groups. Roma were singled out for violence, and police detained numerous individuals for racially motivated attacks against Roma.16
A district court in Kosice decided to restart the trial of a group of police officers accused of abusing in 2009 a group of Romani boys ages 10 to 16, arrested for robbing a woman. The accused did not agree with continuing the trial after one of the judges on the panel had to be replaced. Previously, they also had blocked the proceedings by failing to attend court hearings.17
[20] Despite the negative reporting, the documentary evidence reveals that the state does not condone or acquiesce in the racial and discriminatory behaviour, particularly at the hands of extremists and/or skinheads.
In March approximately 500 LS-NS supporters attended a march in the village of Krasnohorske Pohradie in response to an extensive fire at the nearby Krasna Horka castle, started accidentally by children from the local Romani community. Protesters blamed the entire Romani community and Roma in general for the incident. Police prevented successfully the group from entering the settlement. In April LS-NS leader Marian Kotleba obtained ownership of plots of land in the community where several houses inhabited by Roma were built and announced his plans to "clean up" his land. In September approximately 300 of his supporters attended a gathering in the village, after he called on people to come and help him clean up the "waste" from his land. The police prevented the majority of the group from entering the Romani community; however, they allowed Kotleba, together with a small group of assistants, to enter and survey the land. The LS-NS leader obstructed municipal initiatives to sell municipally owned land to Roma who resided in houses built on the land.18
In September the Mayor of Partizanske supported publicly a march for "the rights of decent people," attended by approximately 300 local residents. The march was aimed against a local Romani community that the non-Roma population perceived to be a source of crime. The march was attended by approximately 200 far-right extremists, who unsuccessfully tried to enter the Romani community. In general the police responded quickly and peacefully to such gatherings and prevented crowds from entering Romani communities or inciting confrontations.19
There are legitimate concerns from some groups that there is impunity for racially motivated attacks and the police responses are not sufficient; however, the preponderance of evidence indicates that the state is taking action against extremists and does not condone or assent to extremist actions. I note there is also some evidence that at the operational level the state is taking action to address the discrimination and violence targeting the Roma population. Despite these efforts by the Slovak government, statistics indicate that Roma are still underemployed and less educated. Critics claim these programs do not go far enough or are just superficial. Even so, the panel finds the preponderance of evidence is that there are programs in place resulting in improvements to the lives of Roma.
Identity
[21] I find that the claimants have established that they are citizens of Slovakia, by way of the passports found in Exhibit 1 and have also established that they are Roma.20
Nexus
[22] For the claimants to be Convention refugees, their fear of persecution must be "by reason of" one of the five grounds enumerated in the Convention refugee definition. I find that the claimants' fear of persecution is linked to one of the five grounds, namely their ethnicity or race, as the claimants established, through their testimony in the Romani language, that they are Roma.
[23] The claimants alleged past persecution (as found above) and fear that, should they return to Slovakia, they would be subjected to those harms in the future, that is, threats, assaults, improper health care, segregated and ineffective schooling, ridicule, humiliation and systemic discrimination in all their dealings with non-Roma Slovakians, and more.
[24] Persecution21 has been defined as a sustained or systemic denial of a core human right. I find that the harm feared does not violate fundamental rights. Also, to constitute persecution, the harm feared must occur with repetition and persistence, or in a systematic way. In Chan,22 La Forest J. (n dissent) reiterated that "[t]he essential question is whether the persecution alleged by the claimant threatens his or her basic human rights in a fundamental way." What distinguishes persecution from discrimination is the degree of the seriousness of the harm.
[25] I find that the harm complained of amounted to an isolated incident or incidents and that, given all the circumstances, there is not a reasonable chance that persecution would take place were the claimants returned to Slovakia.23 The definition of Convention refugee is forward-looking. Claimants must establish that they have a subjective fear of persecution and also that their fear is well-founded in an objective sense.24 Both subjective fear and the objective basis for it are crucial elements in the definition of a Convention refugee.25
[26] I also considered whether the acts of discrimination or harassment alleged may cumulatively constitute persecution. I find that the repeated acts of discrimination and harassment experienced by the claimants do not amount to persecution because the treatment does not amount to a violation of a fundamental right or because the harm complained of was not serious.
[27] Widespread discrimination against Roma continued in employment, education, healthcare, housing, and loan practices. Roma continued to face discrimination in accessing a wide variety of commercial services, including restaurants, hair salons, and public transportation. NGOs asserted that the cases of discrimination reported to legal help lines represented only a fraction of discrimination cases in practice. In many cases Romani individuals from socially excluded communities did not report discrimination. In one case a Romani woman was prevented from entering a nightclub, which allegedly stated on its Facebook page that it did not admit Roma. NGOs continued to report cases of police harassment based on ethnicity.
I also considered whether the acts of discrimination or harassment alleged may cumulatively constitute persecution. I find that the repeated acts of discrimination and harassment experienced by the claimants do not amount to persecution because the treatment does not amount to a violation of a fundamental right or because the harm complained of was not serious. This finding is in keeping with the documentary evidence, all of which speaks of "discrimination" and none that I can find alleges that this discrimination arises to the level of persecution.26 According to Freedom House,27
Slovakia is an electoral democracy. Voters elect the president for up to two five-year terms and members of the 150-seat, unicameral National Council for four-year terms. The prime minister is appointed by the president but must have majority support in the parliament to govern. The March 2012 parliamentary elections were deemed free and fair by international monitors, though the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe expressed concern about a lack of oversight and transparency in campaign financing.
The constitution provides for an independent judiciary. Despite some reforms pushed through by Radicová's government in 2011, including removing 14 judges accused of processing cases too slowly, the court system continues to suffer from corruption, intimidation of judges, and a significant backlog of cases. Prison conditions meet most international standards, but overcrowding remains a concern. NGOs and members of the Romany community report that Romany suspects are often mistreated by police during arrest and while in custody.
The Roma, who comprise roughly 10 percent of Slovakia's population, continue to experience widespread discrimination, including forced evictions and segregation of Romany children in schools. However, in October 2012, a court in Eastern Slovakia ruled that the segregation of Roma in schools is unlawful. In September, the leader of the far-right Peoples Party-Our Slovakia invited his Facebook friends to help him "clean up" a piece of his land by demolishing the dwellings of Roma squatters; police prevented the event from taking place. Slovakia's first-ever Roma representative, Peter Pollak, was elected to the legislature in March 2012 and later became the government proxy for minorities.
[28] My reasons for so finding are set out below, after the section on credibility.
Credibility
[29] In assessing the credibility of the evidence presented by the claimant, I am mindful of the decision of the Federal Court of Appeal in Maldonado28 wherein the Court stated, in part, that "when a claimant swears to the truth of certain allegations, this creates a presumption that those allegations are true unless there be reason to doubt their truthfulness".
[30] The evaluation of the credibility and probative value of the evidence must be made with regard to the totality of the evidence, including what is generally known about the conditions and laws of the country the claimant is from,29 as well as the experiences of similarly situated persons in that country.30 In assessing the claimant's credibility, I have also considered the decision in Orelien31 wherein the Federal Court of Appeal held that it is necessary for the claimant to establish that his or her evidence is probably credible or trustworthy, not just possibly so. I took into consideration the various factors that could affect the claimant's testimony including cultural differences, testimony given through the filter of an interpreter and hearing-room anxiety.32
[31] Claimant's Counsel submitted that the claimants were credible. A mere statement does not make it so. He did not provide any examples of in which way the claimants were credible.
[32] I found the following credibility problems in this claim which caused me to doubt the evidence that has been put forward. The adult female claimant was asked a variety of questions regarding the incident at the hospital, the assault that took place prior to the adult claimants entering the hospital and the claimants' efforts to obtain protection.
[33] The female claimant testified that she and her husband, in XXXX 2011, were on their way home from grocery shopping when they were accosted at a bus stop by five skinheads, who insulted them and physically assaulted them. They jumped her husband, knocked him to the
ground and kicked him. Two of them grabbed her. She screamed and fainted. Then, the five attackers, fearing they had killed the female claimant, fled the scene.
[34] The principal claimant gave the female claimant water. When she revived, they called a friend who took them home. Several hours later, the adult claimants went to the hospital. They were made to wait until all non-Roma Slovaks were treated first.
[35] When asked why they waited for three (3) hours before going to see a doctor, the female claimant testified that they were trying to decide whether to go or not: "They made us wait and did not properly examine us". The claimants asked for "certification" but the health authorities did not provide them with any. I find that, if the incident took place, the injuries to the claimants were minor. If the injuries to the claimants had been significant or severe bodily harm, I find the claimants would not have waited for 3 hours, to decide whether to go to the doctor or not. Further, I find that if the claimants, especially, the principal claimant had suffered serious injury, he would have provided evidence of that from another doctor, either from Slovakia or Canada but he has not. Finally, I find that the lack of corroborating evidence from the health authorities means that the incident likely did not occur, as alleged by the claimants.
[36] The female claimant testified that she noted the assailants had shaved heads, black clothing and distinctive tattoos but did not know them and had never seen them before. However, she said she would recognize them if she saw them again. A police officer treated the claimants shabbily and kicked the principal claimant. When they complained to another officer, they dismissed the complaints, even when they went back a few days later.
[37] The claimants did not make any further attempt to complain to the police about this matter, which means that the claimant does not have a police report of the incident. The claimant does not have any corroborative evidence from the person they allegedly called to drive them home. Furthermore, the claimants do not have a hospital report. Nevertheless, I accept that the principal claimant was assaulted by unknown racists but I do not accept that this incident was either serious or that the claimant made proper efforts to engage the state for the following reasons.
[38] The claimants allege that the health practitioners at the hospital were racist and did not provide them with proper care because they made them wait for several hours, examined them cursorily, and failed to take x-rays, but did provide pain killers. I do not agree. The principal claimant has no evidence that he suffered any serious repercussions from the attack and I find none.
[39] From this testimony, I find that the principal claimant was attacked, suffered minor injuries and that the health system provided the necessary medical care him by providing him with analgesics.
[40] Also, the principal claimant did not know the reason the police officer sent him away or kicked him. The claimant's reasons for not doing more were vague, unconvincing and not cogent. The claimants did not complain to any higher authority regarding the police station in their locale or the local hospital health care practitioners.
[41] I have drawn an adverse inference from this testimony. I shall refer to this evidence below, under various aspects of this analysis. I shall now refer to and analyze the specific allegations, wherein I make further credibility findings.
The Assault, persecution and other risks
[42] The claimants alleged that, between them, they experienced only one physical attack in their adult lives. The principal claimant mentioned he suffered bullying and an assault as a teenager, but the attack in 2011 was the only assault the principal claimant suffered as an adult. He is now 20 years of age. No one should have to suffer any physical attack but I cannot see this one isolated incident to be indicative that the claimants face a reasonable chance of such an attack occurring in the future. I find this was a random assault of opportunity and not a targeted attack against them, personally.
[43] Furthermore, no matter how regrettable it was, the attack had no serious results. The claimant suffered minor injuries, treated by pain killers.
[44] One minor, isolated incident cannot, in and of itself, be considered persecution, as it was not serious, repeated or persistent. Furthermore, one incident leads me to find that, on balance, the claimant does not personally face a risk to life. From this I have drawn an adverse inference.
[45] Another incident that the claimants raised was an attack on the Roma segment of their village by fascists. I noted to the claimants that I had read in the documentary evidence of skinhead violence including home invasions in Slovakia, from 2008 to 2012, provided by the European Roma Rights Centre (ERRC)33 and noted that there was no mention of the "invasion" of their village, although others were. I specifically asked the claimants if they had any news, articles, reports but they did not. As a result, because of the interest of the EU in general and the ERRC in particular, the fact that there was no mention of this allegation means it likely did not occur. From this I have drawn a further adverse inference.
[46] I shall analyse the attack further below when I discuss state protection and whether the claimants suffered cumulative harassment/discrimination amounting to persecution.
Employment
[47] The claimant submitted that he was persecuted because he, specifically, could not and Roma, generally, cannot find jobs.
[48] The female claimant testified in the Romani language. She indicated that she could not speak Slovak very well. I find that it would be unreasonable to expect that a person who does not speak the national language would not have any difficulty in finding employment.
[49] The government of Slovakia provides social assistance. Accordingly, I find that, even if the claimants faced discrimination in employment opportunities, the state provided assistance.
Education
[50] The principal claimant wrote in his BoC narrative that he and his wife attended either segregated classes or segregated schools and were not allowed into 'regular' classes with non-Roma Slovaks. They also alleged that the education provided to Roma is not on par with other Slovaks. The claimants further allege that, should their children return to Slovakia, they would also face segregation in education, which is a form of persecution or cruel treatment.
[51] As far as the adult claimants are concerned, they may have been dealt with differently than non-Roma children, but it is not surprising that they were not enrolled in regular schools when they were not able to speak or understand the language of instruction. Nevertheless, the claimants were provided education. I find that even if it was discriminatory it was not persecutory. Nevertheless, the IRPA is forward-looking. The adult claimants' fears regarding their young children are at best speculative. With the improvements to education noted by the UN HRC (above) and the continuing vigilance of the EU, I find that there is not a serious possibility that the minor claimants will face persecution on this ground, should they return to Slovakia. Even stronger, I find that the minor claimants do not face, on balance, the risk of cruel or unusual treatment in education.
Health
[52] Claimant's Counsel submitted that there is widespread discrimination in health care in Slovakia. He cited the fact that the minor claimant, XXXX, did not receive proper treatment with respect to the XXXXdisease from which he suffers. Nevertheless, the government of Slovakia provided the female claimant with instructions on how to help her child.
[53] As to access to medical care, the claimants have identified they had access and though they were not able to get documents are it relates to them, the minor claimant was properly diagnosed with XXXX XXXX disease and given treatment. Although the female claimant alleged that the doctor did not provide XXXX with proper or comprehensive treatment, there is no evidence that the fears for a child with this rare condition are well-founded.
[54] I do not find the claimants were denied the right to medical coverage or treatment. In fact the evidence is to the contrary. The documents on file indicate that,
In Hungary, Slovakia, Poland, Spain, Portugal and France, medical insurance coverage of around 90 % or more is reported by both Roma and non-Roma respondents. In Italy, about 20 % of both Roma and non-Roma said that they do not have any medical insurance.34
[55] I find the claimants did receive and can expect to receive adequate medical care from the state for the following reasons.
[56] The claimants provided a letter from an XXXX XXXX of Pediatrics and Biochemistry confirming that XXXX XXXX is a patient at the Hospital XXXX XXXX XXXX (Toronto) and "is currently being treated for XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX. XXXX is a XXXX XXXX disease, which, if untreated in infancy, results in irreversible XXXX XXXX."35 However, there is no evidence that the child suffered any adverse effects from his condition. At the close of the hearing, I asked the female claimant if anyone had told her whether her son suffered from an irreversible disability or if he was "okay". She replied, "His results are good and he is OK. This illness depends on the reports." I noted to Claimant's Counsel that there had not been a lot of time to gather more specific information with respect to XXXX XXXX. and then granted time for Claimant's Counsel to provide further medical documents and submissions. I mentioned that only a diagnosis and conditional outcome of the disease had been provided but not the current condition of or the prognosis for the minor claimant, XXXX.
[57] On December 10, 2014, the Claimant's Counsel provided me with a further letter from Professor XXXX.36 This letter explains the XXXX disease, XXXX, in much more detail, including the source (inherited), symptoms, prognosis (life-long and chronic, especially if not treated within a few weeks of birth) and treatment goal, including diet and necessary monitoring. Dr. XXXX concludes by noting that "children with well-controlled XXXX have good outcomes in XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX, while XXXX patients whose blood XXXX levels are frequently outside the treatment targets have XXXX. ... It is also well-known that medical management of XXXX within the first four years of life is of particularly critical importance due to the XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX". I found this letter very informative but it raises more questions than it answers. Notwithstanding that the child, XXXX, is a patient of this facility at XXXX XXXX Hospital, the treating physicians did not provide any indication that this claimant suffered any negative XXXX from his treatment in Slovakia and nothing about his current situation, much less his prognosis. Accordingly, I find that there is no evidence of improper or inadequate treatment by the health care practitioners regarding this claimant in Slovakia. The mother of the child ought to be congratulated for following the health practitioners' instructions and advice after providing her with a proper diagnosis and treatment for her child.
[58] In light of the claimants' allegation of inadequate health care in the face of no evidence of any resulting problem from the treatment of XXXX in Slovakia, I have drawn an adverse inference for the claimants alleging that which is not found to be factually correct, on balance.37
[59] I also find, therefore, that the claimants have not rebutted the presumption of state protection with respect to health care.
Cumulative Discrimination/Harassment
[60] I have also considered whether the acts of discrimination and harassment alleged may cumulatively constitute persecution. I find that the repeated acts of discrimination or harassment these claimants received at the hands of the Slovak people, as reported in their BoCs and as found in their testimony, do not amount to persecution because the treatment complained of does not amount to a violation of a core human right or because the harms feared are not serious.
[61] The UNHCR Handbook dealing with cumulative discrimination, at paragraph 55, reads:
Where measures of discrimination are, in themselves, not of a serious character, they may nevertheless give rise to a reasonable fear of persecution if they produce, in the mind of the person concerned, a fear of apprehension and insecurity as regards his future existence. Whether or not such measures of discrimination in themselves amount to persecution must be determined in the light of all the circumstances. A claim to fear of persecution will, of course, be stronger where a person has been a victim of a number of discriminatory measures of this type and where there is thus a cumulative element involved.
[62] The doctrine of cumulative grounds of persecution was summarized by Mr. Justice Nadon, speaking for the Federal Court of Appeal, in Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Munderere, 2008 FCA 84 (CanLII), 2008 FCA 84, 377 N.R. 259, "Where evidence establishes a series of actions characterized to be discriminatory, and not persecutory, the cumulative effect of that conduct must be considered. It would be an error of law for the RPD not to consider the cumulative nature of that conduct, as directed against the claimant. The RPD is duty bound to consider all the events which may have an impact on a claimant's submission that he or she has a well-founded fear of persecution."
[63] I note that the fact the adult claimants have not learned the Slovak language has led to various problems. If one wants, as the claimants say they do, to maintain their traditions and culture, it is not surprising that they have chosen to live in a Roma part of their village, which may or may not have adequate housing.38 If the claimants' parents did not expose them to or assist them to learn the national language, it is also not surprising that the claimants have a difficulty in dealing with the health system, the police or find good jobs. There is no evidence that the claimants are restricted to living in a Roma ghetto, not learn Slovak or be prevented from higher education because of their ethnicity. There are clearly Slovak-speaking Romas with good jobs and a good command of the Slovak language, from the documentary evidence.
[64] I considered the cumulative effect of incidents of discrimination. I find that the claimants were able to study and work, freedom of movement, lived in a democracy, and although there were problems in certain areas, the Government of Slovakia generally respected the human rights of its citizens. I conclude that the cumulative nature of the events of discrimination did not constitute persecution.
[65] The documentary evidence speaks clearly of discrimination but none of persecution.
State Protection
[66] The issue before me was whether it was objectively unreasonable for the claimants to have sought state protection. While states are presumed to be capable of protecting their nationals, it was open to the claimant, according to the law, to rebut the presumption of protection with "clear and convincing" evidence. I find, on the evidence, that the claimants have not rebutted that presumption, for the following reasons.
[67] According to the US Department of State,
The Slovak Republic is a multiparty parliamentary democracy led by a prime minister and a 150-member parliament (Narodna Rada). Prime Minister Robert Fico heads the Smer-SD (Direction-Social Democracy) party, which secured a majority of seats in the 150-member National Council in the March 2012 early parliamentary elections. President Ivan Gasparovic, the head of state, was re-elected for a five-year term in 2009. Authorities maintained effective control over security forces. Security forces remained committed to curbing human rights abuses.
[68] In Villafranca39, the Federal Court of Appeal considered the issue of state protection in the following terms:
No government that makes any claim to democratic values or protection of human rights can guarantee the protection of all its citizens at all times. Thus, it is not enough for a claimant merely to show that his government has not always been effective at protecting persons in his particular situation. Terrorism in the name of one warped ideology or another is a scourge afflicting many societies today; its victims, however, much they may merit our sympathy, do not become Convention refugees simply because their governments have been unable to suppress the evil. Where, however, the state is so weak, and its control over all or part of its territory so tenuous as to make it a government in name only, as this court found in the case of Zalzali v. Canada (Minister of Employment and Immigration, [1991] 3 F.C. 605, 14 Imm.L.R. (2d) 81, 126 N.R. 126 (C.A.), a refugee may justly claim to be unable to avail himself of its protection. Situations of civil war, invasion or the total collapse of internal order will normally be required to support a claim of inability. On the other hand, where a state is in effective control of its territory, has military, police and civil authority in place, and makes serious efforts to protect its citizens from terrorist activities, the mere fact that it is not always successful at doing so will not be enough to justify a claim that the victims of terrorism are unable to avail themselves of such protection.
[69] Without demanding perfection, what efforts are "serious enough" to allow a finding that state protection for this claimant is adequate? This requires consideration of the actions taken by the state to provide protection and the results of those actions in the context of the nature of the harm feared and the conduct of the claimant to avail himself of state protection.
[70] I note that Prime Minister Fico made racist statements in the run-up to the 2012 parliamentary election.40
Anti-Romani sentiments permeated public and political discourse. In the run up to the March 2012 parliamentary elections, several parties resorted to anti-Romani rhetoric, often painting Roma as a burden on society or referring to "Gypsy parasitism." Mainstream politicians also made derogatory or dehumanizing remarks about the Roma. In February, during remarks to university students, Prime Minister Fico complained about human rights "saints" getting in the way of needed solutions for the Roma. He added that extreme measures, including taking children away from marginalized Romani parents even against their will, could work. Also in February at the Ministry of Interior, Fico repeated that the exceptional situation of the Roma would need serious and exceptional measures.
[71] However, as set out by Justice Harrington in Varga v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2014 FC 510 [Varga] at para 20:
Each case turns on the particular history of the claimant, the record, the adequacy of the analysis by the Tribunal and, indeed, the appreciation of that evidence by various judges of this Court: Banya v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2011 FC 313, [2011] FCJ No 393 (QL), at para 4.
[72] Though the claimants did not seek redress as it relates to the police action when the claimant went to report the incident, I note there are measures in place that the claimants may access. Complaints can be made at local police stations, prosecutor's offices, or at the Ministry of the Interior offices. Roma can follow the same procedures as non-Roma to file complaints. The Ministry of Interior's electronic complaints' form includes sections requesting all information on the unlawful actions of the police force, including:
* description of the event;
* location;
* date and time;
* description of the suspected police authorities, including "name, police personal number, license number plate or other things that can help identify the subject";
* complainant's name, surname, contact address, telephone number or email (ibid. n.d.b).
However, the complainant's name and contact information is "not essential" (ibid.), and the Ministry of Interior states that anonymous submissions will be investigated, although providing a name is encouraged (ibid. n.d.c).
Sources stated that the procedures set to submit a complaint against police are the same for everyone throughout the Slovak Republic (OSF 14 June 2012; Slovak Republic 13 June 2012).41
[73] The panel has considered Counsel's submission that the police and judiciary are failing the Roma people. Unfortunately, no government is expected to guarantee perfect protection to all its citizens at all times. The panel also notes that while there may be a reluctance to test the state's ability to offer assistance, the evidence suggests that there are proactive measures in place to address the discrimination and violence targeting minorities, and specific measures addressing the needs of the Roma population.
[74] The Section of Control and Inspection Services has jurisdiction over all Ministry of Interior units. The Ministry of Interior states that the Section of Control and Inspection Services is a "separate unit" of the ministry, which is "independent from the management and structures of the police" and "subordinated to the Ministry of Interior." According to the Director General of the Section of Control and Inspection Services, a complaint is handled by the head of the authority that the complaint was lodged against, specifically the Regional Police Directorates, Presidium of the Police Force, the Ministry of Interior, and the Section of Control and Inspection Services of the Ministry of Interior (Slovak Republic 4 July 2012). He added that "[t]he aim of the proceedings is to determine the facts of the case and its compliance with the law.42
[75] The documentary evidence states,
63. 2011 saw 243 extremist criminal acts altogether. 107 criminal acts have been solved, representing 44.03 per cent of the total incidence of this type of crime. As for the structure of extremist criminal acts, the following was seen:
- 227 cases of criminal acts of supporting and promoting groups focused on suppressing fundamental rights and freedoms in view of articles 421, 422 paragraph 1, 422a, 422b, 422c, 423 paragraph 1 (a), article 423, paragraph 1 (b) of the Penal Code;
- 6 cases of criminal acts of inciting to nationalistic, racial and ethnic hatred in view of article 424, paragraph 1 of the Penal Code;
- 1 case of the criminal act of incitation, defamation and menace to persons on the grounds of their race, nation, nationality, skin colour, ethnic origin or family origin in view of article 424a, paragraph 1 of the Penal Code;
- 5 cases of the criminal act of violence against a group of inhabitants in view of article 539, paragraph 2 (a) of the Penal Code.
In 2011, the number of racially motivated criminal acts increased by 166, representing a total increase of 127.82 per cent in comparison with 2010.
64. The Police Force has intensified their activity in preventing violence against the Roma, Jewish, and immigrants coming non-EU countries, which was also manifested in a careful monitoring of mass meetings which were summoned by extreme rightist persons. At each mass meeting a police coordination body was called in, while representatives of the town participated as well and they were informed about when and under what lawful circumstances the public process would be held. The mentioned public meetings were in most cases summoned for the purpose of dealing with the 'Roma issue' in relation to a long-term pre-election campaign by the rightist-oriented political party of 'Ludová strana Naše Slovensko' (Folk party - Our Slovakia) whose leader is Mgr. Marián Kotleba, in close cooperation with the civic movement of Slovenská Pospolitost, whose leader is Mr. Marcel Urban. It was also a reaction to conflicts presented in the media between the majority population and the Roma minority (Medunice, Zlaté Moravce, etc).
65. All extremist criminal acts and racially motivated criminal acts have been duly documented and investigated. In order to duly document and investigate these criminal acts, instructional and methodical trainings are held where members of the Police Force are re-trained in new ways of the committing of the criminal activity in question as well as its investigation. In order to ensure that the fight against extremism and racially motivated criminal acts is efficient, the Sub-Department of Extremism and Spectator Violence has been established at the Criminal Police Department of the Police Force Presidium, and each Regional Police Force. Directorate features a Sub-Department of Extremism and Spectator Violence of the Criminal Police Department.43
This is not just actions on paper but speaks to a government that is putting in place actions to address the Roma population problems. Furthermore, I find that the state has implemented its policies and legislation on an operational level to address Roma concerns. The government has created a position of police specialists for the work with the Roma community in order to improve relationship between the Roma and the police. Currently, there are 231 police specialists assigned to undertake these duties. One of the basic tasks of the officers is to patrol areas with increased concentration of Roma or minority communities. Follow-up training for members of the police is currently being conducted in Slovakia, focusing on police action in Roma communities as well as protection of marginalized Roma communities and preventive measure with a view to prevent crime against the Roma communities committed by the police. Members of the police force participate in various programmes, including Romani language course, aimed at the improvement of relations with Roma. During the period under review, educational activities for the police were also conducted by the Slovak National Centre for Human Rights.44
[76] Furthermore, the documentary evidence indicates that there are recourses available to Roma who wish to pursue complaints against the police for inaction. Therefore, according to the evidence which has been provided to the Board, the claimant has not satisfied the burden by providing clear and convincing evidence that, on a balance of probabilities, adequate state protection would not be reasonably forthcoming if the claimants should need it.
[77] The claimants failed to rebut the presumption of adequate state protection with clear and convincing evidence of the state's inability to protect them. In this case, the claimants failed to demonstrate that they had taken all objectively reasonable steps to avail themselves of state protection, and to provide compelling or persuasive evidence to explain their failure to do more than make a single attempt to seek protection from the police. As stated by the Chief Justice of the Federal Court of Canada in Ruszo45 beginning at paragraph 29:
[29] It is settled law that absent a complete breakdown of state apparatus, it should be presumed that a state is capable of protecting its citizens (Ward, above, at para 57). Moreover, "[t]he more democratic the state's institutions, the more the claimant must have done to exhaust all the courses of action open to him or her (Kadenko v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration, [1996] FCJ No 1376, at para 5 (FCA); Avila v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2006 FC 359 (CanLII), 2006 FC 359, at para 30). However, in all cases to which the presumption applies, the burden is upon an applicant for refugee protection to demonstrate, with clear and convincing evidence, the state's inability to provide adequate protection (Ward, above, at paras 57, 59). This burden must be discharged on a balance of probabilities (Carillo v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2008 FCA 94 (CanLII), 2008 FCA 94, at paras 20, 30 [Carillo]).
[30] In discharging this burden, refugee claimants who are outside their country of nationality may demonstrate either that they are "unable" to obtain adequate state protection or that, by reason of a well founded fear of persecution, are unwilling to avail themselves of the protection of their home state. As stated in Ward, above, at para 49:
The distinction between these two branches of the "Convention Refugee" definition resides in the party's precluding resort to state protection: in the case of "inability", protection is denied to the claimant, whereas when the claimant is "unwilling", he or she opts not to approach the state by reason of his or her fear on an enumerated basis.
[31] With respect to the "inability" branch of the definition, it is not sufficient to simply demonstrate that there may have been some local failures of the police to provide state protection (Carillo, above, at paras 32 and 36; Kadenko, above; Avila, above; Rocque v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2010 FC 802 (CanLII), 2010 FC 802, at paras 17-20; Gregor v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2011 FC 1068 (CanLII), 2011 FC 1068, at para 24; Gezgez v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2013 FC 130 (CanLII), 2013 FC 130, at para 11).
[32] An applicant for refugee protection is required to demonstrate that he or she took all objectively reasonable efforts, without success, to exhaust all courses of action reasonably available to them, before seeking refugee protection abroad (Hinzman, above, at para 46; Dean v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2009 FC 772 (CanLII), 2009 FC 772, at para 20; Salamon, above, at para 5). Among other things, this requires claimants for refugee protection "to approach their home state for protection before the responsibility of other states becomes engaged" (Ward, above, at para 25; Kim v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2005 FC 1126 (CanLII), 2005 FC 1126, at para 10 [Kim]; Hassaballa v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2007 FC 489 (CanLII), 2007 FC 489, at paras 20-22); Camacho v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2007 FC 830 (CanLII), 2007 FC 830, at para 10; Del Real v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and (Immigration), 2008 FC 140 (CanLII), 2008 FC 140, at para 44; Ramirez v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2008 FC 1214 (CanLII), 2008 FC 1214, at para 28; Stojka v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2012 FC 1371 (CanLII), 2012 FC 1371, at para 3; Ruiz Coto v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2012 FC 1211 (CanLII), 2012 FC 1211, at para 11; Matthews v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2012 FC 535 (CanLII), 2012 FC 535, at paras 43-45; Kotai v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2013 FC 693 (CanLII), 2013 FC 693, at para 31; Muli v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2013 FC 237 (CanLII), 2013 FC 237, at paras 17-18; Ndoja v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2013 FC 163 (CanLII), 2013 FC 163, at paras 16-18, 25; Dieng v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2013 FC 450 (CanLII), 2013 FC 450, at para 32).
[33] In this regard, doubting the effectiveness of state protection without reasonably testing it, or simply asserting a subjective reluctance to engage the state, does not rebut the presumption of state protection (Ramirez, above; Kim, above). In the absence of a compelling or persuasive explanation, a failure to take reasonable steps to exhaust all courses of action reasonably available in the home state, prior to seeking refugee protection abroad, typically will provide a reasonable basis for a conclusion by the RPD that an applicant for protection did not displace the presumption of state protection with clear and convincing evidence (Camacho, above).
[34] With respect to the "unwillingness" branch, a claimant for refugee protection will not meet the definition of "Convention refugee" where "it is objectively unreasonable for the claimant not to have sought the protection of his home authorities" (Ward, above, at para 56). It bears underscoring that, on this branch of the test, the grounds for failing to seek state protection must be based on well founded fears. ...
[45] The Applicants did not provide a compelling or indeed any reasonable explanation for failing to take any of these steps with respect to the single incident in December 2008 in which they personally were allegedly persecuted and did not receive adequate state protection. In this regard, they did not adduce any evidence to establish that they had any reasonable basis for believing that taking any of these actions might reasonably expose them to persecution, physical harm, inordinate monetary expense, or would otherwise be objectively unreasonable. This distinguishes this case from cases such as Budai, above, where the applicants had a reasonable and compelling basis for fearing the police.
[46] In this context, I am satisfied that the RPD's conclusion falls "within a range of possible, acceptable outcomes which are defensible in respect of the facts and law" (Dunsmuir, above, at para 47). The conclusion was also appropriately justified, intelligible and transparent. On the facts of this case, the RPD's decision was "reasonable in light of the outcome and the reasons" that were given (Newfoundlandand Labrador Nurses' Union v Newfoundland and Labrador (Treasury Board),2011 SCC 62 (CanLII), 2011 SCC 62, [2011] 3 SCR 708, at para 15).
Risks under Section 97
[78] The claimants have not alleged that they face the danger of torture or are at risk of cruel and unusual punishment and no such evidence was adduced. Accordingly, I find that the claimants are not persons in need of protection under either subsection 97(1)(a) or (b)(iii).
[79] In view of my finding that the harms feared do not amount to persecution because they do not violate fundamental rights, I also find that the harms feared cannot amount to a risk to life or to a risk of cruel and unusual treatment, as the harms feared envisaged in all these instances necessarily constitutes a violation of a fundamental right.
[80] For this reason, the claims also fail under section 97 of the IRPA.
CONCLUSION
[81] Having considered all the evidence, and based on the above analysis, I find the claimants are not Convention refugees as the claimants have not established that there is a reasonable chance or serious possibility that they will face persecution for a Convention ground or, on a balance of probability, they will face a risk to life or cruel and unusual treatment or punishment or a danger of torture, believed on substantial grounds to exist, should they return to Slovakia. The Panel rejects these claims pursuant to both sections 96 and 97(1) of the IRPA.
(signed)
"James V. Railton"
James V. Railton
January 16, 2015
Date

5 Wherever the panel has written "I find", "the claimant established" or other such words indicating a finding of fact has been made by the panel, one should read into the finding that it was made on the civil standard, that is to say, on a balance of probabilities and no higher standard. The panel is aware that a claimant has the burden to establish the claim with "clear, convincing and cogent" evidence, which does not raise the standard of proof but merely indicates that a panel must be satisfied the facts in issue are more likely than not to have occurred. Should the panel not be able to determine if a fact in issue is established by the claimant, when it is his or her burden to do so, then the panel will find the fact has not been established. On the other hand, the panel is aware that, once the facts respecting an allegation of persecution is found, then the panel must determine if, on those facts, there is more than a mere possibility of persecution in the future, that is to say, a "reasonable chance" of persecution, as set out in Adjei (infra).
